coredata.dc:title: One-pot room temperature synthesis of orange-emitting carbon dots for highly-sensitive vitamin B12 sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiaosong
Liu, Zhikun
Huang, Yiqian
Zong, Yi
Yang, Xiaoyi
Hu, Zhengqiang
Zeng, Cheng
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the long-wavelength emission have received increasing attention due to their promising application prospects in the biological field. The synthesis of CDs with long wavelength emission mainly focuses on the high temperature method, but the room temperature synthesis is still rarely studied. Herein, a simple room temperature strategy is developed for the preparation of orange-emitting CDs (O-CDs) by Schiff base crosslinking reaction between methyl-p-benzoquinone and triethylenetetramine. The proposed O-CDs show a strong excitation-dependent emission with a relative quantum yield of about 6.56%. Because of the strong inner filter effect, O-CDs can be well applied for the highly-sensitive detection of vitamin B12 (VB12). The intensity ratio (F/F 0) of O-CDs is linear against the concentration of VB12 from 50 nM to 200 μM with a low detection limit of 10 nM. Therefore, the obtained O-CDs nanoprobes provide a promising platform for pharmaceutical analysis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange emitting
Room temperature synthesis
Vitamin B12 sensing
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection process of VB12 by the O-CDs
Synthesis and characterizations of the obtained O-CDs
Analysis of VB12 in three real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the O-CDs. Top inset: diameter distributionof O-CDs. (b) high-resolution TEM image of the O-CDs.
(a) FT-IR, (b) XPS, (c) high-resolution N1s and (d) high-resolution C1s spectra of the O-CDs.

(a) Absorption spectrum, (b) emission spectra at different excitations, (c) 3D FL spectrum and (d) relative quantum yield (rhodamine 6G as a reference) of the O-CDs. The excitation wavelength for measuring quantum yield is set to 460 nm. Concentration of O-CDs: 10 μg/mL.

(a) FL emission change of the O-CDs after adding different concentrations of VB12. (b) Relationship between the F/F 0 value and VB12 concentration. pH: 7.4. Ex: 460 nm. (c) FL response of the O-CDs in the presence of VB12 after the introduction of other vitamins. Concentration of all vitamins: 100 μM. (d) Change of FL intensities of O-CDs in the presence of VB12 after the addition of other common ions. 1, control; 2, Na+; 3, Al3+; 4, Cd2+; 5, Mg2+; 6, Li+; 7, Zn2+; 8, HCO3 −; 9, H2PO4 − and 10, Cl−. Concentration of VB12: 100 μM. Concentration of ions: 100 μM. Ex: 460 nm.pH: 7.4.

(a) Absorption spectra of vitamins, the FL excitation spectrum (yellow) and emission spectrum (purple) of the O-CDs. (b) The FL delays of the O-CDs before and after adding VB12 (5 and 10 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Preparation and detection mechanism of the O-CDs for VB12 sensing.
Comparison of this proposed method with other strategies for the room temperature synthesis of CDs.
Analysis of VB12 in different samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-pot room temperature synthesis of orange-emitting carbon dots for highly-sensitive vitamin B12 sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Yiwu 322000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Yiwu 322000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Research Center, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Yiwu 322000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Research Center, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Yiwu 322000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) are synthesized by a facile room temperature method for highly-sensitive detection of vitamin B12 based on the strong inner filter effect.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the long-wavelength emission have received increasing attention due to their promising application prospects in the biological field. The synthesis of CDs with long wavelength emission mainly focuses on the high temperature method, but the room temperature synthesis is still rarely studied. Herein, a simple room temperature strategy is developed for the preparation of orange-emitting CDs (O-CDs) by Schiff base crosslinking reaction between methyl-p-benzoquinone and triethylenetetramine. The proposed O-CDs show a strong excitation-dependent emission with a relative quantum yield of about 6.56%. Because of the strong inner filter effect, O-CDs can be well applied for the highly-sensitive detection of vitamin B12 (VB12). The intensity ratio (F/F 0) of O-CDs is linear against the concentration of VB12 from 50 nM to 200 μM with a low detection limit of 10 nM. Therefore, the obtained O-CDs nanoprobes provide a promising platform for pharmaceutical analysis applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as the carbon-based nanoaggregate have attracted tremendous interest in recent years. Because of their special optical and electrical properties, CDs have been achieved in a variety of fields such as optoelectronic conversion, photocatalysis, biomedicine, sensing and cellular imaging [1–5]. As compared to dye molecules and toxic cadmium-based quantum dots, fluorescent CDs have been expected to be a very promising optical nanoprobe due to their better biocompatibility and water solubility [6–9].

Currently, the fluorescence (FL) emission of most reported CDs is located in the short wavelength region, such as blue emission [10–13]. Especially for CDs synthesized at room temperature, long wavelength emission is more difficult to achieve. The short wavelength emission of CDs greatly limits their applications because biological samples have the obvious blue autofluorescence [14]. Moreover, the short wavelength light can also damage living cells and biological systems. Meanwhile, the luminescent efficiency of the CDs prepared at room temperature is usually low [15,16], which is not conducive to the use of CDs as optical nanoprobes for the analysis and sensing. Therefore, the room temperature synthesis of highly luminescent CDs with the long-wavelength emission is highly desirable and a great challenge.

Vitamin B12 (VB12) has an important influence on carbohydrate metabolism, DNA synthesis and cell maintenance and so on [17]. VB12 as a water-soluble vitamin cannot be synthesized by the body and exists in various food sources including meat and egg [18]. VB12 intake is closely related to human health. The lack of VB12 can lead to a wide variety of diseases such as fatigue, neurological injuries and heart disease, while the excessive VB12 level can cause folate deficiency, resulting in megaloblastic anemia and oral inflammation [19]. For the last few decades, the detection of VB12 have received increasing attention. Compared with other conventional methods, the FL detection method is facile, rapid, and low cost, which provides a good platform for the detection of VB12 sample.

Methyl-p-benzoquinone was from Shanghai Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. VB12 and triethylenetetramine could be obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Vitamin A (VA), B1 (VB1), C (VC), D3 (VD3), E (VE) and H (VH) were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiment is 18.2 MΩ.cm ultrapure water prepared by EMD Millipore equipment. All reagents were analytic grade without further purification.

The morphology characterization of O-CDs was performed by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The absorption spectra of the as-prepared O-CDs and vitamins were determined by a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer. The element composition of O-CDs was measured with a 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The FL lifetimes of the O-CDs before and after adding VB12 were measured with a FL-TCSPC FL spectrophotometer. The FL spectra of O-CDs were measured with a F97-pro FL spectrophotometer.

The O-CDs could be prepared by a facile room temperature method. Firstly, 10 mg methyl-p-benzoquinone was dissolved in 4.8 mL ultrapure water. Then, 0.2 mL triethylenetetramine was further added. After the totally mixture, the obtained solution was kept for two days at room temperature (about 22 °C). Finally, the dark yellow O-CDs solution with orange emission could be obtained. The obtained product was subjected to dialysis (100–500 MWCO) against ultrapure water over 48 h, followed by vacuum freeze drying to obtain pure O-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection process of VB12 by the O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.1 mL O-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and 0.2 mL different concentrations of VB12 solution were mixed and diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water containing phosphate buffer saline (pH = 7.4). The acquired solution was incubated for 5 min before testing the FL intensity. The FL spectra were determined when exciting at the 460 nm, and the FL emission spectra were recorded between 480 and 750 nm.

In the part of real sample testing, the milk samples are treated based on a reported method with few modifications [20]. Briefly, milk samples are treated with HNO3 and H2SO4. The obtained solution is centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant is adjusted to pH 7.4 and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane for three times to remove protein and lipid moieties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the obtained O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A facile room-temperature method is developed for the synthesis of O-CDs. We firstly focus on the effects of reaction time and the proportion of reactants on the optical properties of O-CDs. Results (Fig. S1) show that the FL intensity of O-CDs first increases and then decreases with the increase of the amount of triethylenetetramine, but there is almost no significant difference in the shape and position of emission peak. Moreover, we also investigate the effect of reaction time on the optical properties of O-CDs. The results (Fig. S2) show that the FL intensity of O-CDs gradually increases as the extension of reaction time and reach a plateau in about 24 h. Based on above these results, reaction time and the proportion of reactants mainly affect the FL intensity of O-CDs. Therefore, the most suitable reaction conditions are set to 200 μL triethylenetetramine and 48 h in the experiment.

The formation of CDs is generally considered to begin with the condensation or polymerization of precursors.[21] Triethylenetetramine as a crosslinker has an important influence on the formation and size of the O-CDs.[22] As shown in Fig. S3, the increase of triethylenetetramine results in larger O-CDs with the diameter increased from about 1 to 7 nm. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) clarifies that the prepared O-CDs under optimal conditions (200 μL triethylenetetramine and 48 h) have diameters ranging from 3 to 9 nm, and their average diameter is approximately 5 nm. The result from high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) further indicates the low crystallinity of the O-CDs because of the lack of clear lattice fringes. The structure of O-CDs is further analysed by FT-IR and XPS spectra. FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows that O-CDs have typical stretching vibrations of NH bond, CO/CN bond and CN bond at 3398, 1568 and 1479 cm−1, respectively.[23,24] XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows that O-CDs have carbon (C1s, 285.06 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.76 eV) as well as oxygen (O1s, 530.74 eV) with an atomic ratio of 65.75/21.07/13.18. Moreover, high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows three peaks at 398.3, 398.9, and 399.8 eV, which are attributed to the CN, N (C)3 and NH bands, respectively [25]. As shown in Fig. 2d, high-resolution C1s spectrum is consisted of four peaks, corresponding to CN/CO (287.5 eV), CO (285.6 eV), CN (285.0 eV), and CC (284.4 eV) groups [26]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Fig. S4) shows two peaks at 530.6 and 531.3 eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO bonds, respectively.

The unique optical properties of the prepared O-CDs are analysed in detail by UV–vis absorption and FL excitation and emission spectra. Fig. 3 a shows that O-CDs have two strong absorption peaks at about 278 and 445 nm, which are ascribed to the π–π* electronic transition of aromatic sp2 carbon and the n–π* electronic transition of CO/CN bonds, respectively.[12] The FL emission of CDs is usually considered to come from quantum confinement effect or surface state. The low crystallinity indicates that the FL of O-CDs is mainly attributed to their surface state. FL spectra (Fig. 3b) reveal that O-CDs have an excitation-dependent emission, and the emission wavelength will gradually redshift as the increase of the excitation from 320 to 520 nm. And the maximum emission wavelength of O-CDs is located at 580 nm when exciting by 460 nm (Fig. 3 c). The excitation-dependent emission property indicates the uneven surface state of O-CDs. Chen et al. [27] demonstrate that CO and CN related surface defects are responsible for the multi-state absorptions and tunable emissions of the CDs. The O-related groups (CO) and N-related groups (CN) can introduce two new energy levels and then produce new electron transitions. Thus, the tunable FL emission of O-CDs may be attributed to the synergistic effect of CO and CN bonds. Meanwhile, O-CDs show concentration-dependent FL properties (Fig. S5). The FL intensity of O-CDs increases with the increase of O-CDs concentration from 0.001 to 10 μg/mL. However, with the further increase of O-CDs concentration from 10 μg/mL to 10 mg/mL, the FL emission of O-CDs decreases rapidly and is accompanied by a significant red shift, which caused by reabsorption effect at high concentration [28]. Therefore, to eliminate the reabsorption effect, 10 μg/mL is a suitable concentration of O-CDs for the characterization.

Moreover, the relative quantum yield (rhodamine 6G as a reference) of O-CDs is about 6.56% (Fig. 3d), with a bright orange FL emission under UV light. Combining the FL lifetime (1.66 ns), the radiative rate (k r) of the O-CDs is 3.95 × 107 s−1 by using this following formula:
k r
= ϕ / τ
wherein k r refers to the radiative rate constant, while ϕ and τ refer to the quantum yield and FL lifetime, respectively. Such high radiative rate suggests that high electronic transition may exist in the O-CDs, which attributes to the high FL efficiency. Currently reported CDs synthesized at room temperature usually emit short wavelength blue emission with a low quantum yield (Table 1 ), which greatly limits their further applications. Although the quantum yield of the proposed O-CDs is only 6.56%, it is still much higher than that of most other room temperature-driven CDs. Therefore, these good optical properties enable O-CDs to be used as an optical probe for analytical applications.

VB12 as the quencher can efficiently quench the FL emission of the prepared O-CDs. With the increase of VB12 content, the quenching efficiency of O-CDs increases gradually. The orange FL intensity of O-CDs can be quenched by about 70% after the introduction of 0.2 mM VB12 (Fig. 4 a), indicating the strong quenching ability of VB12. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship between the FL intensity ratio (F/F 0) of O-CDs and VB12 concentration in the ranges of 0.05–50 µM and 50–200 µM can be achieved (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, the limit of detection using this sensing platform is as low as 10 nM (3σ/K). Moreover, the detection of VB12 using the O-CDs is highly specific. Some common vitamins including VA, VB1, VC, VD3, VE, and VH have no interference with the FL of O-CDs (Fig. 4c), indicating that they will not interfere with the detection of VB12. Moreover, some ions have also no any effect on the FL intensity of O-CDs after the addition of VB12 (Fig. 4d). These results show that O-CDs nanoprobes have a good selectivity for the detection of VB12. In the IFE-based sensing systems, theoretically, as long as the absorption of the interferents overlaps with the excitation/emission spectra of the luminescent probe, the determination of the analyte can be affected. Biological media usually have strong blue spontaneous FL with maximum excitation in the UV region.[31] Therefore, the O-CDs with orange emission have a negligible interference from the background autofluorescence of biological tissues.

The highly-efficient FL quenching of the O-CDs after the addition of VB12 can be ascribed to the strong IFE. As shown in Fig. 5 a, VB12 has a strong and wide absorption below 600 nm, causing a great spectral overlap with the excitation peak (yellow curve) and emission peak (purple curve) of the O-CDs. The large spectral overlap causes a significant reduction in the FL efficiency of O-CDs. On the contrary, other vitamins do not influence the FL of O-CDs, because their absorption spectra do not overlap with the excitation and emission bands of O-CDs. This result indirectly proves that the FL quenching of O-CDs by VB12 is based on the strong IFE between O-CDs and VB12. FL lifetime is used to further analyse the VB12-induced FL quenching mechanism of O-CDs. In the IFE-based sensing system, the introduction of the analytes will not affect the lifetime of luminescent probes [32–35]. Fig. 5b shows that the FL lifetimes of O-CDs keep unchanged before and after adding VB12, which further reveals that the VB12 caused FL quenching of O-CDs is due to the IFE mechanism.

In addition, Stern–Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) is used to analyse the FL quenching efficiency of O-CDs: [36].
(1)
F 0
/ F =
K SV
×
q
+
1
wherein F 0 is the FL intensity of O-CDs solution, F is the FL intensity of O-CDs after adding VB12, K SV is the quenching constant (M−1) as well as q is the VB12 concentration. The F 0/F value of the O-CDs is linear with the molar concentration of VB12 (F 0/F = 0.00755 c VB12 per μM + 1.00388, R2 = 0.99), thus the corresponding quenching constant of K SV is 1.14 × 104 M−1. Meanwhile, the FL quenching type can be further studied by the K SV. The K SV can be considered as a ground-state association constant for the O-CDs combining to VB12 in static quenching effect. And K SV in dynamic quenching effect is part of the following equation (Eq. (2)): [37].
(2)
K SV
=
k q
×
τ 0
wherein τ0 is the lifetime of the O-CDs (τ0 = 1.66 ns) and k q is quenching rate constant. By the calculation, the value of k q is about 6.87 × 1012 M−1 s−1, revealing a static quenching effect occurs in the detection of VB12 by O-CDs becasue the dynamic quenching is generally less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of VB12 in three real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the proposed sensing platform to determine the VB12 content in three real samples (lake water, fetal bovine serum and milk samples). Before the detection, different concentrations of standard VB12 solution (1, 10, and 50 μM) are introduced into the O-CDs. Results show that the recoveries of VB12 are between 97.9% and 108.8% (Table 2 ), indicating the accuracy and reliability of the constructed analysis method.

In conclusion, we develop a one-pot room temperature method for the synthesis of the O-CDs. The prepared O-CDs have an excitation-dependent orange FL with a relative quantum yield of about 6.56%. Meanwhile, the O-CDs as a FL nanoprobe can be well used to detect the VB12 with a wide linear range from 0.05 to 200 μM. Because of the good recovery and high reproducibility, O-CDs nanoprobes have a great prospect in the detection of VB12 in complex samples.

Xiaosong Huang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhikun Liu: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yiqian Huang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yi Zong: Visualization, Investigation. Xiaoyi Yang: Visualization, Investigation. Zhengqiang Hu: Visualization, Validation. Cheng Zeng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Jinhua Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2021-4-325 and 2021-4-178), Science and Technology Program of Yiwu Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 21-3-14) and Key Program of Provincial Natural Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LZ22H180003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121239.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
